content: Emerging infectious diseases (EIDs) are newly recognized infections or those increasing in incidence/geographic range. ~75% are zoonotic (animal-to-human spillover). Drivers: land use change (deforestation, urbanization), wildlife trade, climate change (expanding vector ranges), antimicrobial resistance, global travel/trade. Recent examples: SARS-CoV-1 (2003), MERS-CoV (2012), Ebola outbreaks, Zika (2015-16), SARS-CoV-2 (2019). Spillover risk factors: high human-wildlife contact (bushmeat hunting, wet markets), intermediate hosts amplifying virus, viral characteristics (RNA viruses higher mutation rates, broad host range). Surveillance systems: WHO Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN), CDC Epidemic Intelligence Service, ProMED-mail, HealthMap digital surveillance. One Health approach integrates human, animal, environmental health.

Outbreak response framework: 1) Detection and verification (case identification, laboratory confirmation); 2) Risk assessment (transmissibility, severity, spread potential); 3) Containment measures: case isolation, contact tracing and quarantine, infection prevention/control, social distancing if needed; 4) Medical countermeasures: therapeutics (repurposed drugs, monoclonal antibodies, convalescent plasma), vaccines (platform technologies enable rapid development); 5) Communication: transparent risk communication, combat misinformation; 6) International coordination under International Health Regulations (IHR 2005). Travel restrictions generally limited utility, may delay spread. Healthcare system preparedness: surge capacity, PPE stockpiles, negative pressure rooms. Post-outbreak: after-action reviews, system strengthening.

EID diagnosis challenges: unknown pathogen requires unbiased detection methods. Approaches: 1) Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) from clinical specimens identifies novel pathogens without prior knowledge; 2) Pan-viral/pan-bacterial PCR panels detect broad pathogen classes; 3) Serology for exposure history; 4) Electron microscopy for viral morphology. Biosafety considerations: BSL-3/4 containment for high-consequence pathogens; appropriate PPE; sample inactivation protocols. Pathogen discovery pipeline: sequence → phylogenetic analysis → culture isolation (if possible) → diagnostic assay development → therapeutic/vaccine targets. Genomic epidemiology tracks transmission chains and evolution in real-time. Data sharing critical: GISAID for influenza/SARS-CoV-2 sequences, GenBank for broader pathogen genomics.
